model_findings.description: The `--host` flag accepts any value (e.g. `0.0.0.0`) with no `--allow-remote` guard or warning, despite docs claiming the proxy is localhost-only. Binding to a non-loopback interface exposes an unauthenticated subprocess-spawning endpoint to the LAN. This is the exact gap tracked in issue #2030 and should be enforced in code, not just docs.

`ChatCompletionRequest` has `extra=allow` and no max length on `messages` or message `content`. A client can POST arbitrarily large bodies that get flattened into a single prompt string and piped to a CLI subprocess (codex/claude/gemini/hermes), causing memory blowup and long-running subprocesses. FastAPI has no default body size limit. Cap message count, per-message content length, and total flattened prompt size before invoking the backend.

`POST /v1/chat/completions` spawns local CLIs (codex exec, claude, gemini, hermes) with no authentication, no API key check, and no rate limit. Any process on the host (including a browser via DNS rebinding when bound non-loopback, or any local user) can drive paid model usage and consume subprocess slots. At minimum require a shared-secret header before invoking a backend.

`/healthz` synchronously spawns four `--version` subprocesses per request with a 1s timeout each (worst case ~4s wall, 4 forks). Unauthenticated callers can trivially DoS the host via repeated probes; this is the issue noted as #2029. Cache probe results for N seconds or run probes out-of-band.

Route handlers are declared `def` (sync) and invoke `subprocess.run` with up to a 120s timeout while holding a worker thread. Concurrent requests serialize behind uvicorn's default threadpool (40 threads) and easily exhaust it, blocking even `/healthz`. Use `async def` + `asyncio.create_subprocess_exec`, or enforce a small concurrency semaphore.

`_codex_backend` creates a NamedTemporaryFile with `delete=False` before the `try` block. If `NamedTemporaryFile` itself succeeds but an exception is raised between the `with` exit and `try:` (e.g. KeyboardInterrupt or unlikely allocation failure), the file leaks. Minor, but moving the assignment into the `try` would close the window. Also note `tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False)` in a `with` block is an antipattern — use `mkstemp` for clarity.

Gemini and Hermes backends pass user-controlled prompt via `--prompt=...` / `--oneshot=...` flag syntax. While argv-array invocation prevents shell injection, a prompt beginning with `-` or containing newlines could still be misinterpreted by future CLI flag parsing changes. Prefer stdin-piped prompts (as codex does) or explicit `--` separator where supported.

The best-practices doc says the Grok route uses `hermes -z PROMPT -m grok-4.3`, but the implementation calls `hermes --oneshot=PROMPT -m grok-4.3`. Either the doc or the code is wrong; reconcile so on-call doesn't chase phantom invocation differences.

`finish_reason` is hardcoded to `"stop"` even when a backend was killed for timeout or returned truncated output. Real OpenAI clients (LangChain, LiteLLM) branch on `length`/`content_filter`. Acceptable for Phase 1 but worth a TODO.

model_findings.description: When activity_schema fails, the function returns immediately via _python_qg_report before any other gate runs (word_count, plan_sections, vesum_verified, etc.). Downstream consumers and the writer-correction loop receive a gates dict containing only tool_theatre and activity_schema; any code that iterates expected gate keys or aggregates failures across gates will see absent entries rather than 'failed' entries. The pre-existing flow computes gates['passed'] = all(...) over present gates — here gates['passed'] is set to False directly, but other gates that would also have failed are invisible, which weakens diagnostic value and may surprise reviewers/UIs that expect a stable gate-key set.

Items that are not Mapping (e.g. a bare string, list, or null emitted by a malformed writer) are skipped with no violation recorded and do not increment `checked`. Likewise, an error-correction activity with `items` missing or not a list passes the gate silently. A writer regression that emits `items: ['Я прокидаєшся...']` (strings) — a plausible failure mode for the very class of bugs this gate targets — would bypass detection entirely. Recommend emitting a violation for non-mapping items and for missing/empty `items` on types with a strict schema.

Field comparison is strictly case-sensitive. A writer emitting `Error:`, `ERROR:`, or camelCase `Sentence:` would be flagged as 'forbidden field' with the generic message rather than the more helpful alias-suggestion message, missing an opportunity to nudge the writer to the canonical lowercase form. Adding case-folded alias lookup (with original-case offending_field reported) would improve diagnostic quality without weakening the gate.

Plausible writer aliases not in the table — `correct_form`, `correctText`, `target`, `solution`, `errorText`, `wrongWord` — would still be rejected by the whitelist, but with a generic 'do not allow field' message that doesn't tell the writer which canonical field to use, weakening the writer-correction loop's ability to self-repair. Since the stated goal (per PR context) is to catch alias drift the model invents, broadening this map (or making the gate emit a hint when the field name fuzzy-matches `error`/`correction`) would directly address the failure mode the gate is intended to guard.

If an item has only `incorrect:` and `correction:` (no `sentence:`), the alias `incorrect` → `error` adds `error` to aliased_required, so the missing-required check correctly still reports `sentence`. However, the item produces two violations (forbidden `incorrect` + missing `sentence`) which is correct but worth a test: there is no test covering the missing-`sentence` path, only missing-`error`. A regression that relaxes the required set would slip past current tests.
